paragraphs.body: ​ It’s in the middle of a suburban neighborhood at the end of a cul-de-sac, a run-down abandoned barn sits looming over the nearby houses on a small hill. Trees shroud the surrounding area, providing cover if you wanted to sneak into the barn. The whole barn is made of dark brown wood, weathered and beginning to rot. The front of the barn is plain with a gaping front door leading. Through the front door lies an empty room filled with organic and inorganic waste littering the ground. On the right side is an opening to a smaller room with similar features, and immediately on the right is a staircase leading to the lower level of the barn. On the lower level, the ground is littered with broken bottles, the floor is made of concrete and the lower level is lined by pens previously used to house the animals. Unused planks of wood fill the baskets previously used to feed the animals, and the overgrown vegetation on the open side of the underground section creeps its way into the pens. ​ The sun was just beginning to dip below the leaves of the trees, coloring the sky in slow strokes of gold, lavender, and orange, when the scent of charcoal and roasting sweet corn drifted lazily through the thick summer air. I was barefoot in the grass, its cold blades dancing on my toes, keeping me present as laughter swirled around me—my cousin trying to catch fireflies in a jar held aloft, my dad singing off-key to the beat of an old soul tune on the hot Bluetooth speaker, the pop and fizz of a soda can opening near the grill. I bit into a slice of watermelon, its sticky sweetness running down my chin and onto my forearm, and for a suspended moment, time slowed. The whir of the cicadas buzzed like a distant lullaby, the overhead string lights flickered to life, and a gentle breeze rustled the distant sound of children splashing in the neighbor's pool. My mother summoned my name across the yard, smiling softly as she patted the seat beside her on the porch swing. I took a seat beside her, allowing the soft creak of the swing and the warmth of her arm around my shoulders to calm me into peaceful contentment. There was no event, no news, no occasion—just the golden quiet of a summer evening. The Cabin sat in a dark corner behind the Cathedral of Learning, surrounded by mowed grass cut low. There were numerous, albeit scattered, trees around the place providing a somewhat shadowed aura to the cabin, despite all the leaves on the trees having fallen off long ago. Cathy, sitting not far behind the cabin, towered over it like a tree towers over a person. The cabin was made of wood, most of it painted. It was clear that the place was old, as the wood had a dark hue to it almost like it was damp. The wood had a striped design, with dark brown lines running horizontally along the cabin, separated by what appeared to be unpainted sections. The roof was intact, albeit filled with sticks and other tree parts. There are no roads leading to the cabin, only a sidewalk that almost leads to it, but veers away to go towards Cathy. It had a chimney poking out of its left side when viewed from the front, lined with wood containing the same design the rest of the cabin has. A humble porch juts out from the front of the house, covered by an overhang that reaches up only slightly below the bottom of the roof. The door is old, and appears as if moving it would make a screeching creak like in horror movies. It had metal supports connecting the door to the hinges, suggesting it was falling apart. Windows sit on either side of the door, lined with green wooden shutters, and in surprisingly good condition.

paragraphs.body: It was a lukewarm rainy day, I was riding my motorcycle with a friend and we were going to one of the spots that we liked to hangout and enjoy the view. I was riding behind him and he was beginning to make a left hand turn at a three way intersection. I for a split second was not paying attention and noticed a moment too late I needed to brake. My right hand and leg slammed on both brakes with such suddenness it felt like they teleported. Unfortunately due to braking too hard, a piece of debris on the road, and the lack of traction due to the rain, my bike lost balance and it and I began to slide out. Time seemed to slow as i tumbled to the ground, rolling along on top of my motorcycle as if we were in a race, my arms were extended outwards as if I was desperated trying to grasp onto a rock while falling. I had thought in those fractions of a second, “is this where I die?” After the dust had settled, I was laying on the cold asphalt back to the ground, my left foot trapped under my 500Ibs motorcycle and unable to move. Thankfully, I was wearing heavy duty boots so my left foot was not hurt. My friend upon realizing what had happened screamed obseneites in out comms system we had set up in our helmets, and rushed to my aid so hastily he had forgotten to use his kickstand to stop his bike from falling over. After lifiting my bike off my foot and we retreated to the side of the road, I assessed myself for damages. There were two deep circular wounds right next to each other on the side of my elbow, and some minor road rash on my hips. Needless to say I got extremely lucky in this crash. With no serious injuiries on me, me and my friend decided to head home. After some help from locals to get back on our feet, we started our journey home with a fear so deep in us, you could see the trembles in my hand reflected by the wobbling of the steering bars. Every turn, every stop, every point we slowed down at we made sure to call it out in our comms system to make certain there was not a chance that could happen again. When I got home, I tended to my wounds and took a long rest. ​ Thrill is that electric moment when everything in your body feels alive, alert, and just on the edge of something wild or extraordinary. It’s the rush you get before a rollercoaster drops, or when your heart pounds as you’re about to speak in front of a crowd. Thrill is feeling the strong wind blowing on you as you ride a motorcycle at 60 MPH or higher. Thrill is going to an abandoned location for the first time not knowing if you’ll get hurt from a fall or an unexpected inhabitant, as well as the rush of adrenaline when you hear a car pull up while you’re somewhere you’re not supposed to be. A thrill isn’t always tied to danger—it can come from laughter so hard your stomach aches, or from hearing a song that makes you want to dance no matter where you are. It’s the spark that cuts through the dullness of routine and jolts you into feeling fully present, even just for a second. Thrill is unpredictable, fleeting, and always powerful; it doesn’t ask permission, it just surges through you and reminds you that you’re alive. ​ An inconspicuous decommissioned railway leads the way to the tower. The closer you are to the tower, the more its gargantuan height becomes apparent, taking up more of your field of view until you have to look straight up at the sky to see its top. To the right, there is a staircase leading to a concrete structure containing an object so rusted and withered, it’s impossible to tell what it’s supposed to be. Past the structure is a relatively small lake, in the center of which a structure is visible poking out of the surface of the water. This place used to be a quarry before it hit a waterline and was promptly abandoned. On the right side of the tower stands a spiral staircase leading to the top. Its rust was omnipresent, the guard rails were withering away, so compromised that you could physically shake them loose if you wanted to. At the top, you can see for many miles, and you can easily see the entire lake. On the inside, two walls wrap around on themselves into two cylinders, sectioning off most of the first floor. Both walls have large holes poked in the side of them, and looking through you see the gaping void that is the lower interior of the tower. Towards the back of the room is another staircase leading to the highest floor of the tower. One side of the wall is completely missing, providing a beautiful albeit dangerous view of the surrounding area. Next to that is a cramped hallway containing what appeared to be maintenance areas and various funnels leading to who knows where.
